query: role of CADM1 in myelodysplastic syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Myelodysplastic Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) is a diverse collection of hematologic neoplasms characterized as a clonal disorder of hematopoietic stem cells, resulting in dysplasia and ineffective hematopoiesis within the bone marrow. This condition often leads to various degrees of cytopenias, which can manifest as anemia, leukopenia, or thrombocytopenia. Some individuals with MDS may progress to acute myeloid leukemia (AML), further complicating their clinical course. The prognosis for patients with MDS is highly variable and is influenced by factors such as cytogenetic abnormalities and the severity of cytopenias. Specifically, patients exhibiting the 5q chromosomal deletion tend to have a more favorable prognosis compared to those with monosomy 7, necessitating careful monitoring and tailored management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: Myelodysplastic Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Granulocytic precursors may have hyposegmentation and hypogranulation. [5] Quantification of myeloblasts must number less than 20% on bone marrow evaluation, as greater than 20% indicates acute myeloid leukemia. [8] Myeloblasts, or immature myeloid cells, are noted to have a high nucleus-to-cytoplasm ratio, fine nuclear chromatin, and visible and prominent nucleoli, with or without granules. Auer rods, which are pink/red rod-like structures in the cytoplasm of blasts, are uncommon in MDS but can be seen in MDS with excess blasts-2. [5] Relevant immunostains are performed, such as myeloperoxidase (MPO), CD34, CD117, CD61 or CD42b for megakaryocytes, CD68 for monocytes, CD20 for B-cell lineage, and CD3 for T-cell lineage. [5] CD34 is an antigen expressed on progenitor and early precursor cells, a marker for blasts. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Myelodysplastic Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Those patients who are classified as high-risk or have an unfavorable prognosis probably require treatment and may be considered for an allogeneic stem cell transplant to maintain their remission. However, even favorable risk patients with MDS may still have considerable morbidity and mortality from their disease. About a third of patients also have transformation into AML, and these patients often have a very poor prognosis. [8] Patients with isolated 5q deletion might experience longer survival than other types of MDS, with one study noting 5-year survival of 40% if they did not receive treatment and 54% if they received treatment. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Histone Code -- Clinical Significance -- Current FDA Approved Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: Azacytidine and decitabine are the two FDA approved drugs that inhibit DNA methyltransferases and result in reduced DNA methylation. Azacytidine was approved for the treatment of myelodysplastic syndromes by the FDA in 2004, and decitabine received approval for the treatment of myelodysplastic syndromes in 2006. [8] Histone Deacetylase Inhibitors

retrieved_docs.title: Shwachman-Diamond Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Routine blood workup can provide information about cytopenias. Patients with SDS need frequent monitoring of blood for early detection of progression to myelodysplastic syndromes or acute myelogenous leukemia (AML).

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Marrow Failure -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Gene sequencing in other disorders (Shwachman-Diamond syndrome, congenital amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia, DBA, reticular dysgenesis) is mandatory because there are no screening tests. Blackfan-Diamond anemia is associated with increased serum adenosine deaminase (ADA) levels. [3] [4] [6] [5] [8] Other causes of bone marrow failure are acquired. The most common forms occur from drugs, chemicals, radiation, viral infections, immune disorders, MDS, PNH, or large granular lymphocytic leukemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Basophilic Stippling -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Megaloblastic anemia due to vitamin B12/folate deficiency or chronic alcohol use disorder can present with basophilic stippling as well. [4] Furthermore, myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) acquired from variable gene mutations over time induces faulty erythropoiesis and hematopoietic stem cell differentiation. Numerous morphological defects are present on peripheral smears from patients with MDS, and basophilic stippling is a common finding. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Myelodysplastic Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Over 100 somatic point mutations have been implicated in MDS, and there is some overlap with AML. The most common somatic alterations include mutations in TET2 , SF3B1 , ASXL1 , DNMT3A , SRSF2 , RUNX1 , TP53 , U2AF1 , EZH2 , ZRSR2 , STAG2 , CBL , NRAS , JAK2 , SETBP1 , IDH1 , IDH2 , and ETV6. These mutations have been shown to correlate with various features. TP53 mutations are associated with complex cytogenetics and poor overall survival. RUNX1 and TP53 tend to correlate with worse thrombocytopenia. TET2 mutations have a better response to hypomethylating agents. [8] The World Health Organization (WHO) 2016 Classification of Myeloid Neoplasms describes several subcategories of myelodysplastic neoplasms and overlap syndromes that have both myeloproliferative and myelodysplastic features. This classification is based on differences in morphology, dysplasia, and karyotype, particularly 5q deletion. [1] The classification of MDS by WHO (2016) is as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Myelodysplastic Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism for the development of MDS has been implicated by various genetic and chromosomal abnormalities, which may occur de novo or secondary to 1 of the above etiologies. Cytogenetic abnormalities are seen in more than 80% of patients, including translocations or, more commonly, aneuploidy (loss or gain of a chromosome). [8] Changes in cytogenetics play a large role in the International Prognostic Scoring System. Deletion of the long arm of chromosome 5 (5q) is the most common abnormal karyotype and may be subdivided into 2 categories: treatment-related MDS with 5q deletion, usually with exposure to alkylating agents, versus de novo isolated 5q deletion. Patients with 5q deletion related to prior chemotherapeutic agents usually also have other cytogenetic abnormalities and/or TP53 mutations and usually portend a poor prognosis. Isolated 5q deletion without other cytogenetic abnormalities has a significantly better prognosis. Other cytogenetic abnormalities commonly studied include normal karyotype, deletion 7q (-7), trisomy 8, and -Y. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Leukocyte Adhesion Deficiency -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The leukocyte adhesion deficiency prognosis varies depending on the severity of the disease; it is usually fatal before one year of age. Moderate LAD cases can live longer than the third decade of life with appropriate antimicrobial therapy. Those patients with successful allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplants can have a better quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Pure Red Cell Aplasia -- Pathophysiology -- Congenital PRCA (Diamond-Blackfan Anemia)
retrieved_docs.content: The first gene identified belonged to a Swedish patient who carried a balanced translocation between chromosomes X and 19. [12] The disease is equally prevalent in both sexes, essentially ruling out chromosome X's role. Subsequent linkage analyses involving European families identified chromosome 19q13 as the site involved in DBA's pathophysiology. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Aplastic Anemia -- Pathophysiology -- Clonal Evolution
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia may progress to paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH), myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS), or acute myeloid leukemia (AML) due to genetic alterations and chromosomal irregularities. [26] [27] [28] [29] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Myelodysplastic Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Screen patients at risk for myelodysplastic syndrome, including those with a history of chemotherapy or radiation exposure, to enable early intervention. Identify the clinical signs and symptoms of myelodysplastic syndrome to facilitate early diagnosis. Differentiate between various subtypes of MDS based on cytogenetic findings and hematologic parameters to guide treatment. Communicate modalities to improve care coordination among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients affected by myelodysplastic syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Evaluation of Tumors -- Clinical Significance -- Myeloid neoplasms
retrieved_docs.content: The IHC profile of myeloid sarcoma varies depending on the degree of differentiation (monocytic versus granulocytic) and maturation (blasts versus maturing myeloid cells) within the myeloid lineage. Neoplastic cells generally express CD43, CD68, and myeloperoxidase (~90%). Myeloid neoplasms with monocytic differentiation often lack CD34 or CD117, whereas those with granulocytic differentiation typically express these markers. Additionally, approximately 40% of myeloid sarcomas exhibit abnormal cytoplasmic nucleophosmin 1 (NPM1) expression, which is associated with monocytic differentiation. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2004
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Abnormal” is used as an interpretation of فقر دم فانكوني (Fanconi anemia), indicating that the clinical and laboratory findings associated with this genetic disorder are considered abnormal. Fanconi anemia is a hereditary condition characterized by bone‑marrow failure, congenital anomalies, and a heightened risk of malignancies, and the descriptor “Abnormal” reflects the pathological nature of its presentations.

Recognizing this relationship is important for accurate diagnosis and targeted management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2441
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Abnormal” is used as an interpretation for both the Arabic phrase “البزل السّلوي غير الطبيعي” and the condition “فقر دم فانكوني.” In clinical practice, describing a spinal tap as “البزل السّلوي غير الطبيعي” indicates an abnormal cerebrospinal fluid result, while labeling “فقر دم فانكوني” as abnormal reflects the presence of Fanconi anemia, a hereditary bone‑marrow failure syndrome. Recognizing that “Abnormal” serves as a common descriptor for these distinct entities highlights its role as a generic marker of pathological or atypical findings in patient evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1800
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is identified as a specific subtype of فقر الدم (anemia), indicating that it falls under the broader category of anemia disorders. In clinical practice, فقر الدم (anemia) is frequently associated with متلازمة فانكوني (Fanconi syndrome), a renal tubular disorder that can lead to impaired reabsorption of nutrients and electrolytes, contributing to the development of anemia. Consequently, patients with Fanconi syndrome may present with various forms of anemia, including acute posthemorrhagic anemia when hemorrhagic events occur, highlighting the importance of recognizing the link between renal tubular dysfunction and hematologic abnormalities for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 751
retrieved_docs.content: Crizanlizumab is identified as a selective adhesion molecule inhibitor, placing it within the product class of selective adhesion molecule inhibitors. In addition, crizanlizumab is classified as both an immunomodulatory agent (imunomodulátory) and an immunosuppressive agent (imunosupresiva), reflecting its dual capacity to modify and dampen immune system activity. These properties align crizanlizumab with the broader disposition of immunomodulators, indicating that its therapeutic action involves regulation of immune responses through targeted inhibition of adhesion molecules. This integrated classification underscores crizanlizumab’s role in modulating immune pathways and its potential utility in conditions where controlling immune cell adhesion and activity is clinically beneficial.

retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, which is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hereditary hemoglobin disorder that can precipitate serious respiratory complications in adults. Clinically, داء الكريات المنجلية is associated with respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and adult cardiac arrest, underscoring the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt intervention in patients affected by this disease.
